title-group.article-title: Digital Biometric Measures in Long COVID
title-group.subtitle: A Secondary Analysis of the STOP-PASC Randomized Clinical Trial
title-group.alt-title: Digital Biometric Measures in Long COVID
Digital Biometric Measures in Long COVID

article-meta.aff: 1Stanford Quantitative Sciences Unit, Stanford University School of Medicine, Stanford, California
2Department of Medicine, Stanford University School of Medicine, Stanford, California
4University of Wisconsin School of Medicine and Public Health, Madison
6AI/ML Quantitative Digital Sciences, Global Biometrics and Data Management, Pfizer Inc, Cambridge, Massachusetts
7Biomeasures, Endpoints and Study Technologies, Pfizer Research and Development, Pfizer Inc, Cambridge, Massachusetts
8Department of Internal Medicine, University of Iowa Carver College of Medicine, Iowa City

author-notes.title: Article Information
author-notes.p: Open Access: This is an open access article distributed under the terms of the CC-BY-NC-ND License, which does not permit alteration or commercial use, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies. © 2025 Gunturkun F et al. JAMA Network Open.
author-notes.corresp: Corresponding Author: Linda N. Geng, MD, PhD, Department of Medicine, Stanford University School of Medicine, Stanford, California, 211 Quarry Rd, Ste 301, Palo Alto, CA 94304 (geng@stanford.edu).
author-notes.p: Author Contributions: Drs Gunturkun and Hedlin had full access to all of the data in the study and take responsibility for the integrity of the data and the accuracy of the data analysis.
Concept and design: Gunturkun, Hedlin, Bonilla, Demanuele, Cai, Yang, Singh, Geng.
Acquisition, analysis, or interpretation of data: Gunturkun, Hedlin, Botzheim, Deng, Jagannathan, Quach, Kim, Lin, O'Riordan, Tzeng, Adamowicz, Demanuele, Cai, Yang, Singh, Geng.
Drafting of the manuscript: Gunturkun, Bonilla, Kim, Geng.
Critical review of the manuscript for important intellectual content: Gunturkun, Botzheim, Deng, Jagannathan, Quach, Lin, O'Riordan, Tzeng, Adamowicz, Demanuele, Cai, Yang, Singh, Geng.
Statistical analysis: Gunturkun, Hedlin, Botzheim, Deng, Kim, O'Riordan, Tzeng, Adamowicz, Demanuele.
Administrative, technical, or material support: Gunturkun, Jagannathan, Quach, Lin, Cai, Yang, Singh, Geng.
Supervision: Gunturkun, Hedlin, Bonilla, Jagannathan, Cai, Yang, Singh, Geng.

Conflict of Interest Disclosures: Dr Hedlin reported receiving institutional support from Pfizer and grants from National Institutes of Health (NIH) outside the submitted work. Dr Bonilla reported receiving grants from Pfizer during the conduct of the study. Dr Jagannathan reported receiving grants from Pfizer during the conduct of the study. Ms O'Riordan reported receiving personal fees from AiCare during the conduct of the study. Mr Adamowicz reported being an employee of and owning stock in Pfizer during the conduct of the study. Dr Demanuele reported being an employee of and owning stock in Pfizer during the conduct of the study. Dr Cai reported being an employee of Pfizer during the conduct of the study and receiving personal fees from Pfizer outside the submitted work. Dr Yang reported being the founder of and stockholder in AiCare outside the submitted work. Dr Singh reported receiving grants from Pfizer during the conduct of the study. Dr Geng reported receiving grants from Pfizer during the conduct of the study; grants from Agency for Healthcare Research and Quality and NIH outside the submitted work; and personal fees from Clearview Healthcare Partners and Speakers Network outside the submitted work. No other disclosures were reported.
Funding/Support: This study was funded by Pfizer Inc.

Role of the Funder/Sponsor: Authors of this substudy who were employed by the funder had a role in the design of the study, interpretation of the data, and review and approval of the manuscript. The funder had no role in the conduct of the study; collection, management, and analysis of the data; preparation of the manuscript; and decision to submit the manuscript for publication.

Additional Contributions: We thank John Sammuelsson, PhD, and Jiawei Bai, PhD, Pfizer Inc, for their input and support on this project. They did not receive direct compensation for their contributions to this work. We thank the participants, their caregivers, and the research team and leadership of Stanford Center for Clinical Research.
Additional Information: The code is accessible on GitHub at https://github.com/fatmagunturkun/Digital-Biometric-Measures-in-Long-COVID.

permissions.copyright-statement: Copyright 2025 Gunturkun F et al. JAMA Network Open.

license.license-p: This is an open access article distributed under the terms of the CC-BY-NC-ND License, which does not permit alteration or commercial use, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.

abstract.p: This secondary analysis identifies changes in physical activity, heart rate, and oxygen saturation as tracked by a wearable device between participants with postacute sequelae of SARS-CoV-2 infection who received nirmatrelvir-ritonavir vs placebo-ritonavir treatment.

sec.title: Question
sec.p: What are longitudinal digital wearable biometric measures and trajectory patterns in participants with postacute sequelae of SARS-CoV-2 infection (or long COVID) enrolled in a randomized clinical trial?

sec.title: Findings
sec.p: In this substudy of a randomized clinical trial involving 50 participants, distinct longitudinal trajectories of digital biometric measures for long COVID were identified, including physical activity and heart rate.

sec.title: Meaning
sec.p: The findings suggest that physiological and behavioral biometric measures from wearable devices are promising digital biomarkers for use in long COVID clinical trials and research.

sec.title: Importance
sec.p: Digital biometrics can be used to monitor disease, but there is limited research on their applications for assessing postacute sequelae of SARS-CoV-2 (PASC) or long COVID.

sec.title: Objective
sec.p: To better understand digital biometric patterns in long COVID using wearable device technology and whether there are any differences between the nirmatrelvir-ritonavir and placebo-ritonavir intervention arms.

sec.title: Design, Setting, and Participants
sec.p: This secondary analysis is an exploratory substudy of a placebo-controlled randomized clinical trial (Selective Trial of Paxlovid for PASC [STOP-PASC]) that was conducted from November 2022 to September 2023 at Stanford University. Trial participants were randomized, and a subset enrolled into the prespecified substudy.

sec.title: Intervention
sec.p: Participants were randomized 2:1 to receive oral nirmatrelvir (300 mg) and ritonavir (100 mg) or placebo-ritonavir twice daily for 15 days. Substudy participants were provided with a smartwatch and asked to wear it for 24 hours a day, 7 days a week for the 15-week study.

sec.title: Main Outcomes and Measures
sec.p: Mean changes in digital biometric measures in physical activity, heart rate, and oxygen saturation tracked by a smartwatch. Biometric measures were summarized by treatment arm for each of 5 different time frames: baseline, treatment period, and 3 subsequent time intervals. Summary measure trajectories were clustered and demographics and clinical characteristics were compared among clusters using absolute standardized differences expressed in units of SDs.

sec.title: Results
sec.p: Of the 94 participants enrolled in the substudy, 50 (37 in nirmatrelvir-ritonavir and 13 in placebo-ritonavir) met the analysis eligibility criteria based on wear time and data completeness. These participants had a mean (SD) age of 42.7 (13.2) years and included 29 females (58.0%). Using mixed models for repeated measures, no significant differences were detected between the intervention arms in the change in biometric measures over time, consistent with the patient-reported outcomes in the STOP-PASC trial. In the overall substudy cohort, latent class mixed models and cluster analysis identified distinct longitudinal trajectories of long COVID over the 15-week study that tracked with different symptoms. Participants with lower daytime physical activity reported more severe fatigue (9 of 9 [100%] vs 21 of 23 [91.3%]; absolute standardized difference [ASD], 0.30), shortness of breath (9 of 9 [100%] vs 7 of 23 [30.4%]; ASD, 1.31), and cardiovascular symptoms (8 of 9 [88.9%] vs 12 of 23 [52.2%]; ASD, 0.70). Those with higher nighttime physical activity reported more gastrointestinal symptoms (2 of 3 [66.7%] vs 11 of 35 [31.4%]; ASD, 0.50). Additionally, participants with higher median daytime heart rates reported less fatigue (7 of 9 [77.8%] vs 39 of 40 [97.5%]; ASD, 0.63) and shortness of breath (3 of 9 [33.3%] vs 23 of 40 [57.5%]; ASD, 0.50) compared with those with lower heart rates.

sec.title: Conclusions and Relevance
sec.p: This secondary analysis identified distinct longitudinal trajectories of physiological and behavioral digital biometric measures captured from wearable devices that reflect the heterogeneity and track with different symptoms of long COVID. Digital biometric measures from wearable devices have promising utility for long COVID research.

sec.title: Trial Registration
sec.p: ClinicalTrials.gov Identifier: NCT05576662

sec.title: Introduction
sec.p: Postacute sequelae of SARS-CoV-2 infection (PASC), also known as long COVID, is a chronic condition that is present for at least 3 months after SARS-CoV-2 infection and can manifest with a multitude of symptoms impacting function and quality of life.1,2 Some features of long COVID, such as tachycardia, can be quantified in the clinical setting, but most of the prominent symptoms such as fatigue, postexertional malaise, and shortness of breath are subjective and present a challenge for tracking outcome measures of long COVID but may have surrogate or proxy measures via noninvasive digital sensor technologies.3 Multimodal digital data can be used to inform, quantify, and augment subjectively captured nonpathological fatigue measures4 and have also shown promise in other conditions with similar clinical features to long COVID, such as chronic fatigue syndrome or myalgic encephalomyelitis.5,6 There is an urgent need for validated objective measures for assessing long COVID, and digital biomarkers have great potential utility for drug development and establishing therapeutic efficacy outcomes.7

The use of wearable devices to monitor physiological parameters have enabled estimation of early manifestations of disease and detection of changes that occur during acute and convalescent phases of infectious illnesses.8,9,10 Wearable device technology has been shown to detect changes in heart rate, daily step count, and time asleep at least 9 days before COVID-19 clinical symptoms were reported and to detect increases in heart rate per step after symptom onset.11,12 The use of a health monitoring device (ie, Biostrap; Biostrap USA) in individuals who recovered from COVID-19 compared with uninfected control individuals demonstrated a decrease in light and deep sleep time.13 A recent study found that resting heart rate changes measured by wearable devices persisted for more than 12 weeks in individuals with long COVID, with greater history of physical activity being protective against long-term changes.14

There is limited longitudinal data for wearable device biometrics in individuals with long COVID and, to our knowledge, no published wearable device data as part of outcome measures in a clinical trial to test therapeutic efficacy.6,13,15 The Selective Trial of Paxlovid for PASC (STOP-PASC) randomized clinical trial did not find a benefit from 15 days of nirmatrelvir-ritonavir compared with placebo-ritonavir for improving 6 symptoms in participants with moderate to severe long COVID.16 In this exploratory substudy of the STOP-PASC trial, we leveraged a technology platform that collected biometric data from a wearable device in participants with long COVID.17,18 Our objective was to better understand digital biometric patterns in long COVID and whether there are any differences between the nirmatrelvir-ritonavir and placebo-ritonavir intervention arms.

sec.title: Methods
sec.p: The Stanford Institutional Review Board approved the STOP-PASC trial, including this prespecified substudy. Written informed consent was obtained from all participants. We followed the Consolidated Standards of Reporting Trials (CONSORT) reporting guideline.

sec.title: Study Population
sec.p: The STOP-PASC main trial was previously described.16 Briefly, it was a single-center, randomized, blinded, placebo-controlled trial to evaluate the efficacy and safety of nirmatrelvir-ritonavir in treating long COVID in adults and to explore potential biological and digital wearable biometric markers of disease. A total of 155 participants with long COVID were randomized 2:1 to treatment with oral nirmatrelvir-ritonavir (300 mg/100 mg) or placebo-ritonavir twice daily for 15 days. A subset of participants with an iPhone 6S Plus or newer opted to enroll in the wearable device substudy (Supplement 1). These participants were provided with a smartwatch (Apple Watch Series 4 or newer; Apple) and asked to wear it on their wrist 24 hours a day 7 days a week throughout the entire 15-week study period, except when it was charging or during water activities if preferred.

The trial’s sample size was based on the primary end point. The sample size for this exploratory substudy was driven by the overall trial sample size and the number of eligible participants who enrolled in the substudy and wore the provided smartwatch. This substudy was conducted from November 2022 to September 2023 at Stanford University.

sec.title: Digital Platform
sec.p: We used the Active Intelligence Platform (AiCare Corporation) for data collection, and it consisted of the following: (1) smartwatch (Apple Watch Series 4 or newer) synced to a smartphone (iPhone); (2) smartphone (iPhone 6S Plus or newer; Apple); (3) Cloud-enabled, Health Insurance Portability and Accountability Act–compliant server (Amazon Web Services); (4) web-based open Application Programming Interface architecture; and (5) smartphone application (AiCare iPhone application).17,18 This comprehensive platform was deployed in a mobile operating system (iOS; Apple) environment to analyze the participants’ physical activity, heart rate, heart rate variability, respiratory rate, oxygen saturation, electrocardiogram, and blood pressure. This platform provided secure and timely data tracking and collection.
As a measure of engagement with the device, we generated a wear time parameter. This parameter is described in eMethods in Supplement 2.

sec.title: Outcomes
sec.p: The outcomes assessed in this substudy included mean changes in various digital biometric measures of physical activity, heart rate, and oxygen saturation captured by a smartwatch. The activity signal is a cumulative measurement of physical activity, reflecting the total physical activity of the participants (eMethods in Supplement 2). From this signal, we derived the following features for each valid day. First was the percentage of time in low physical activity (daytime). Low activity was defined as the percentage of time during the daytime when activity levels dipped below the first quartile of the baseline distribution across all participants. Second was the percentage of time in high physical activity (daytime and nighttime). This time was defined as the percentage of time during both daytime and nighttime when activity levels exceeded the third quartile of the baseline distribution. Third was relative physical activity (daytime), which was calculated as (% high activity – % low activity) / (% high activity + % low activity) × 100. This measure indicates the balance between high and low activity levels. Values closer to +100 indicate more high activity, while values closer to −100 indicate more low activity. Values around 0 indicate a balance between the high and low activities. Fourth was peak physical activity (daytime and nighttime) defined as the mean activity level during the 15 minutes of highest activity level.

Step counts were assessed using the total number of steps taken during the entire 24-hour period. Heart rate end points included median, SD, 5th percentile, 95th percentile, resting heart rate median, walking heart rate median, and heart rate variability median. Oxygen saturation outcomes included median and 5th percentile values. We calculated these end points separately for daytime and nighttime signals.

sec.title: Statistical Analysis
sec.p: Our primary analysis included participants who had at least 1 valid day during the baseline period and 2 valid days per week for the remaining 14 weeks, allowing up to 4 missing weeks. Biometric measures were summarized by treatment arm for each of 5 different time frames: baseline, treatment period, and 3 subsequent time intervals (eMethods in Supplement 2).

sec.title: Comparison Between Treatment Arms
sec.p: Mixed models for repeated measures (mmrm package, version 0.3.12, in R; R Project for Statistical Computing) were used to compare weekly mean changes in end points from baseline between treatment arms.19 Each model was adjusted for age, sex, and the baseline mean of the corresponding summary measure (equation 1 in the eMethods in Supplement 2). An AR1 covariance structure was used. We presented the weekly difference between the mean change from baseline of summary measures between the nirmatrelvir-ritonavir and placebo-ritonavir arms, along with the 95% CI for the difference.

sec.title: Clustering of Summary Measures Trajectories
sec.p: Latent class mixed models (lcmm package, version 2.1.0 in R) were used to identify distinct groups of participants with similar longitudinal summary measure patterns for each outcome measure.20 Absolute values of the outcome measure at each time point, including baseline, were modeled as a function of time.21 This model accommodates random intercepts to account for correlated measures within the individuals and group-specific natural cubic splines to model changes in biometric measures over time, allowing for a flexible representation of longitudinal data. We evaluated different numbers of knots and clusters using the bayesian information criterion and compared it with the Akaike information criterion (eMethods in Supplement 2). Among the 3 models with the lowest bayesian information criterion or Akaike information criterion, the simplest (considering the number of classes and knots) was chosen as the final model. The estimated group trajectories, along with CIs for summary measures over the 15-week follow-up period, were displayed. Observed individual summary measures within each cluster were visualized using heatmaps. Following the identification of clusters, we compared the demographics and other clinical characteristics among the clusters using absolute standardized differences (ASDs), expressed in units of SDs.22 Race and ethnicity were self-reported by patients and included the categories Asian, Black or African American, Hispanic, White, and other (including multiracial and unknown). These data were collected to characterize the demographics of the study population.

All tests were 2-sided with α = .05. The statistical models relied on a flexible assumption regarding missing data—ie, that the data were missing at random or missingness was related to observed features only. Analyses were performed using R, version 4.2.1 (R Project for Statistical Computing).23 A sensitivity analysis was conducted as described in eMethods in Supplement 2.

sec.title: Study Population
sec.p: A total of 94 participants were initially enrolled in the wearable device substudy, and 50 participants had eligible data to be included in the primary analysis, of whom 37 were assigned to the nirmatrelvir-ritonavir arm and 13 to the placebo-ritonavir arm (Figure 1). Among the 50 participants analyzed, 29 (58.0%) were female and 21 (42.0%) were male, with a mean (SD) age of 42.7 (13.2) years, and 4 (8.0%) identified as Asian, 2 (4.0%) as Black or African American, 6 (12.0%) as Hispanic, 40 (80.0%) as White, and 2 (4.0%) as multiracial (Table 1). In the nirmatrelvir-ritonavir arm, there was a higher proportion of female participants (24 of 37 [64.9%] vs 5 of 13 [38.5%]; ASD, 0.55) and participants were older on average (mean [SD] age, 43.9 [14.5] years vs 39.4 [7.8] years; ASD, 0.40) compared with the placebo-ritonavir arm. The mean (SD) duration between the index infection and randomization was 16.4 (9.7) months, with the nirmatrelvir-ritonavir arm having a longer duration than the placebo-ritonavir arm (17.3 [10.0] months vs 13.8 [8.8] months; ASD, 0.38). Obesity was more prevalent in the placebo-ritonavir vs nirmatrelvir-ritonavir arm (6 of 13 [46.2%] vs 12 of 37 [32.4%]; ASD, 0.42). At baseline, the placebo-ritonavir arm had a higher proportion of participants experiencing fatigue (13 of 13 [100%] vs 34 of 37 [91.9%]; ASD, 0.42) and gastrointestinal symptoms (7 of 13 [53.8%] vs 14 of 37 [37.8%]; ASD, 0.33). Conversely, the nirmatrelvir-ritonavir arm had a higher prevalence of body aches (23 of 37 [62.2%] vs 5 of 13 [38.5%]; ASD, 0.49) (Table 1). Wear durations over time for each participant in the primary study population are depicted in eFigure 1 in Supplement 2.

tr.td: Months from index infection to randomization, mean (SD)
Symptoms, with moderate or severe ratingc

table-wrap-foot.p: Abbreviations: ASD, absolute standardized difference; BMI, body mass index (calculated as weight in kilograms divided by height in meters squared).

fn.p: A larger ASD indicates a larger difference between the arms (eg, 0.2 = small difference, 0.5 = moderate difference, and 0.8 = large difference).
Race and ethnicity were self-reported by participants.

Symptom severity rating was based on a 4-point Likert scale, with 0 indicating none, 1 indicating mild, 2 indicating moderate, and 3 indicating severe). The percentage of participants with moderate and severe symptoms is presented.
Cardiovascular symptoms include chest pain, dizziness, heart racing, fast heart rate, or palpitations.

sec.title: Comparing Biometrics Between Treatment Arms
sec.p: Biometric measures were similar between the treatment arms at baseline (Table 2). During the baseline period, there were no significant differences in daytime activity levels between the nirmatrelvir-ritonavir and placebo-ritonavir arms, with the median (IQR) percentage of time spent in high activity being 23.9% (18.9%-30.6%) for the nirmatrelvir-ritonavir arm and 23.6% (15.6%-30.3%) for the placebo-ritonavir arm. Similarly, the median (IQR) percentage of time spent in low activity was 25.3% (20.1%-36.3%) for the nirmatrelvir-ritonavir arm and 28.1% (23.4%-30.4%) for the placebo-ritonavir arm. Nighttime activity followed a similar pattern, with high activity levels of 20.2% (14.5%-31.7%) for the nirmatrelvir-ritonavir arm vs 22.2% (16.9%-26.5%) for the placebo-ritonavir arm. The median (IQR) step count over a 24-hour period was slightly lower in the nirmatrelvir-ritonavir arm at 4145 (2584-6452) steps compared with 4784 (3389-7435) steps for the placebo-ritonavir arm.

caption.title: Summary Measures by Treatment Arm in the Primary Study Population

table-wrap-foot.p: Abbreviations: HR, heart rate; HRV, HR variability; O2, oxygen.

sec.p: No statistically significant changes were observed at any time point during follow-up in any measure after adjusting for age, sex, and the baseline mean of the biometric measure in the mixed models for repeated measures model. While the nirmatrelvir-ritonavir arm tended to exhibit slightly greater changes from baseline compared with the placebo-ritonavir arm in most weeks, these differences did not reach statistical significance (eFigures 2 and 3 in Supplement 2).

sec.title: Sensitivity Analysis
sec.p: For a higher stringency population with more complete wear time, 37 eligible participants were included and had similar baseline characteristics to those of the primary analysis population (eTables 1 and 2 in Supplement 2). Temporal patterns of biometric measures in the high stringency population were similar to those observed in the primary population, and no difference was observed between treatment arms (eTable 3 in Supplement 2).

sec.title: Physical Activity Clusters
sec.p: First, we examined daytime activity trajectories. The percentage of time spent in high physical activity during the daytime clustered into 3 distinct trajectory groups (Figure 2). The group of participants who were minimally active throughout the study reported more severe symptoms, including fatigue (group 1: 9 of 9 [100%] vs group 3: 21 of 23 [91.3%]; ASD, 0.30) and shortness of breath (group 1: 9 of 9 [100%] vs group 3: 7 of 23 [30.4%]; ASD, 1.31), and cardiovascular (group 1: 8 of 9 [88.9%] vs group 3: 12 of 23 [52.2%]; ASD, 0.70) symptoms. Group 1’s mean (SD) 95th percentile of heart rate was notably lower (group 1: 93.8 [7.1] vs group 3: 102.1 [13.4]; ASD, 0.53). There were no significant differences in the proportion of participants receiving nirmatrelvir-ritonavir treatment across these 3 trajectory groups (Table 3). The percentage of time spent in low physical activity during the daytime also revealed 3 distinct groups, where the group with the highest percentage of sedentary time throughout the study consisted of participants with fatigue (eFigure 4 in Supplement 2). Relative physical activity also consisted of 3 trajectory groups. Group 1 compared with group 3 was characterized by higher mean (SD) percentage of time in low activity (35.3% [7.7%] vs 19.1% [4.3%]; ASD, 1.85) relative to high activity (11.4% [3.1%] vs 31.2% [4.5%]; ASD, 3.47) and consisted primarily of younger participants (mean [SD] age, 37.8 [11.7] years vs 44.1 [12.7] years; ASD, 0.33) who reported fatigue (5 of 5 [100%] vs 24 of 26 [92.3%]; ASD, 0.28), shortness of breath (5 of 5 [100%] vs 10 of 26 [38.5%]; ASD, 1.13), and cardiovascular symptoms (5 of 5 [100%] vs 15 of 26 [57.7%]; ASD, 1.06) (eFigure 5 in Supplement 2). For peak physical activity during the daytime, 2 groups were identified, one of which contained only 1 participant. Due to the high similarity in peak physical activity levels among the participants, the model could not distinguish subgroups meaningfully (eFigure 6 in Supplement 2).

caption.title: Distinct Clusters of Daytime Percentage of Time in High Activity
caption.p: In panel B, each row represents a patient.

caption.title: Characteristics of Distinct Clusters of Percentage of Time in High Activity

table-wrap-foot.p: Abbreviations: ASD, absolute standardized difference; BMI, body mass index (calculated as weight in kilograms divided by height in meters squared); HA, high activity; HR, heart rate; LA, low activity; PA, physical activity.

sec.p: Next, we examined nighttime activity trajectories. Percentage of time spent in high physical activity during the nighttime revealed 3 groups, where the most active nocturnal group experienced higher mean (SD) 95th percentile of heart rate (group 3: 81.2 [3.9] vs group 1: 71.2 [10.2]; ASD, 0.72) and reported more gastrointestinal symptoms (group 3: 2 of 3 [66.7%] vs group 1: 11 of 35 [31.4%]; ASD, 0.50) (eFigure 7 in Supplement 2). For nighttime peak physical activity, all participants in the group with the highest peak physical activity reported fatigue, and the majority reported body aches (group 3: 4 of 5 [80.0%] vs group 1: 15 of 25 [60.0%]; ASD, 0.34), cardiovascular (group 3: 4 of 5 [80.0%] vs group 1: 14 of 25 [56.0%]; ASD, 0.35), and gastrointestinal (group 3: 4 of 5 [80.0%] vs group 1: 6 of 25 [24.0%]; ASD, 0.86) symptoms (eFigure 8 in Supplement 2).

sec.title: Heart Rate Clusters
sec.p: Daytime median heart rate revealed 2 trajectory groups, where participants in the group with higher heart rates throughout the study were generally younger (mean [SD] age, group 2: 37 [9.8] years vs group 1: 44 [13.7] years; ASD, 0.58), were predominantly female (group 2: 7 of 9 [77.8%] vs group 1: 21 of 40 [52.5%]; ASD, 0.55), and reported less fatigue (group 2: 7 of 9 [77.8%] vs group 1: 39 of 40 [97.5%]; ASD, 0.63) and shortness of breath (group 2: 3 of 9 [33.3%] vs group 1: 23 of 40 [57.5%]; ASD, 0.50) compared with those with lower heart rates (eFigure 9 in Supplement 2). The 5th percentile daytime heart rate clustered into 4 groups, with older (mean [SD] age, group 1: 57.2 [18.9] years vs group 4: 43 [12] years; ASD, 0.63) and male (group 1: 5 of 5 [100%] vs group 4: 1 of 10 [10%]; ASD, 1.63) participants comprising more of the group with lower 5th percentile heart rates (eFigure 10 in Supplement 2). The 95th percentile daytime heart rate clustered into 2 groups, which included 46 and 3 participants (eFigure 11 in Supplement 2).

Nighttime median heart rate clustered into 4 groups. The group with higher nighttime heart rates tended to be younger (mean [SD] age, group 4: 38.4 [13.3] years vs group 1: 51.7 [16.9] years; ASD, 0.50), to be female (group 4: 4 of 5 [80.0%] vs group 1: 0 of 6 [0%]; ASD, 1.50), and to report cardiovascular symptoms (group 4: 5 of 5 [100%] vs group 1: 2 of 6 [33.3%]; ASD, 0.94). Conversely, the group with lower nighttime heart rates tended to be older, to be male, and to report fewer symptoms (eFigure 12 in Supplement 2). For the nighttime 5th percentile heart rate, 2 groups were identified, where participants in the group with lower 5th percentile heart rates predominantly reported fatigue (group 1: 42 of 43 [97.7%] vs group 2: 4 of 6 [66.7%]; ASD, 0.89) and shortness of breath (group 1: 25 of 43 [58.1%] vs group 2: 1 of 6 [16.7%]; ASD, 0.95) (eFigure 13 in Supplement 2). For the nighttime 95th percentile heart rate, 3 groups were observed, where participants in the group with lower 95th percentile heart rates tended to be older (mean [SD] age, group 1: 47 [13.9] years vs group 2: 39.3 [11.5] years; ASD, 1.21) and to report more brain fog (group 1: 21 of 24 [87.5%] vs group 3: 1 of 2 [50.0%]; ASD, 0.58) (eFigure 14 in Supplement 2).

To determine whether concomitant cardiac medications might influence the observed differences in heart rate trajectories, we analyzed the use of β-blockers (metoprolol, propranolol, atenolol, labetalol, carvedilol, bisoprolol, and sotalol), calcium channel blockers (diltiazem, amlodipine, and verapamil), and stimulants (mixed amphetamine salts). A total of 11 participants were taking at least 1 of these medications, and there were no significant patterns in medication use across the groups (eFigures 9-14 in Supplement 2).

sec.title: Discussion
sec.p: To our knowledge, this substudy is the first longitudinal wearable device study tracking digital biometric measures in a randomized clinical trial testing treatment in long COVID. We found no clear differences in digital biometric outcomes over time between nirmatrelvir-ritonavir and placebo-ritonavir, which is consistent with the primary patient-reported outcomes (PROs) previously reported.16 Given the similarity between the intervention arms, we assessed the study cohort as a whole to investigate overall biometric trajectories over time and found several interesting patterns in behavioral and physiological signals that tracked with different symptoms of long COVID.

We identified distinct longitudinal physical activity and heart rate patterns during daytime and nighttime. Lower daytime physical activity was associated with more severe symptoms of fatigue, shortness of breath, and cardiovascular symptoms and corresponded with lower heart rate, suggesting a more sedentary and disabling state. Higher nighttime activity was associated with more gastrointestinal symptoms and increased heart rate variability. Similarly, those with high peak activity levels at nighttime reported a range of symptoms, including fatigue, body aches, and cardiovascular issues, suggesting a less restful night and disturbed sleep. Regarding heart rates, higher daytime median heart rates were observed in a predominantly younger and female group, reporting fewer symptoms. During the nighttime, lower heart rates were observed in a predominantly older and male group. These patterns suggest different physiological subgroups or health statuses within the cohort.

Gupta et al6 previously identified 2 distinct clusters with low and high trajectories for step count, heart rate, and other biometric measures over time following the diagnosis of COVID-19. The study cohort exhibits similar characteristics to this low trajectory profile, indicating that participants generally aligned more closely with the lower activity and biometric markers observed in the study.6 According to the findings by Banach et al,24 our cohort’s step counts suggest a heightened mortality risk, placing them within the higher risk zones, although the step count observed in our study cohort also aligns with the prepandemic mean step count in the US, reported by Tison et al25 as approximately 4819 steps per day. Heart rate and oxygen summary measures for our cohort were overall within normal ranges at baseline.

sec.title: Strengths and Limitations
sec.p: This study has several strengths, including longitudinal data collection for over 3 months and multidimensional data correlation with PROs. However, it also has several limitations. Only individuals with an iPhone with iOS 6S or higher were eligible for inclusion, and participants were mostly local to the study site, which limits the generalizability of our findings to the broader population. This longitudinal study is also limited by data missingness from some participants and by the overall small sample size. Because this is a hypothesis-generating exploratory substudy, the sample size was driven by voluntary opt-in from the STOP-PASC trial, which was powered for the primary outcome, and we did not adjust for multiple comparisons; therefore, the results should be interpreted within this context. Findings that persist for several weeks or across biometrics should be confirmed and further studied in future work. This study used remote monitoring with consumer devices and, as indicated by McConnell et al,26 encountered technical and logistic challenges due to participants forgetting to wear devices, incorrect setup, or failure to sync or share data. Additionally, the inability to adjust measurement times and the reliance on built-in algorithms in proprietary devices for metrics, such as heart rate variability, can limit the generalizability of research findings. These factors could substantially affect the outcomes and should be carefully considered in the design of future studies. These clinical biometric measures over time do not have corresponding real-time activity or subjective reporting from participants; therefore, we do not know if a decrease in activity is driven by symptoms such as postexertional malaise or volitional activity change. Additionally, this study lacked a true preintervention baseline, and future studies would benefit from incorporating a run-in period to better capture a physiological baseline.

Future studies should incorporate patient-flagged events that can be tagged to the biometric data timeline so that granular symptom-biometric association and assessments can be conducted, including for events that trigger postexertional malaise. Additionally, systematic association between larger, more complete digital biometric datasets and validated PROs for long COVID will be important along with tying to other biomarkers to create a multimodal model of long COVID measures.

sec.title: Conclusions
sec.p: In this substudy of the STOP-PASC randomized clinical trial that involved participants with long COVID, we identified distinct longitudinal trajectories based on digital biometric measures from a wearable device, reflecting the heterogeneity of long COVID and providing insights into behavioral and physiological correlates to patient-reported symptoms. Our study findings support the use of digital wearable biomarkers as a promising surrogate tool for assessing long COVID outcomes. Larger studies are needed to confirm and further investigate these digital biometric subgroups and their relationship with phenotypic and biological subtypes of long COVID. Multidimensional integration of digital biometric, clinical, and biological data will offer avenues for deeper understanding of long COVID and for exploring biomarker end points for clinical trials in long COVID.
